topic: Internal course: Investigative Study of a Religious Practice
subject: religious-studies

items.q: What is the difference between emic and etic perspectives?
items.correct: Emic is the insider's perspective — how practitioners themselves understand their practice; etic is the outsider's analytical perspective using academic frameworks.
items.misconception: Emic means the researcher's own academic interpretation of the practice.
items.random_wrong: Emic and etic are two categories of religious ritual.
items.why: Students reverse the terms or assume the scholarly view is 'emic'; keeping insider (emic) and outsider (etic) straight is essential for methodology marks.

items.q: Is a textbook account a primary source for studying a religious practice?
items.correct: No — primary sources are first-hand: observations, interviews, original scriptures or artefacts. Textbooks and scholarly articles are secondary sources interpreting them.
items.misconception: Anything published in a reputable book counts as a primary source.
items.random_wrong: Primary sources are any sources written before the year 1900.
items.why: Students judge sources by authority rather than proximity; the primary/secondary distinction rests on first-hand versus interpretive material.

items.q: Should an investigative study judge whether the religion's beliefs are true?
items.correct: No — the task is to investigate the meaning and significance of a practice for its practitioners, using balanced, empathetic analysis rather than verdicts on truth claims.
items.misconception: A good investigative study must conclude whether the religion is right or wrong.
items.random_wrong: The study must end with the researcher joining the practice.
items.why: Students confuse evaluation of significance with judging truth; the assessment rewards understanding practitioners' perspectives, not apologetics or debunking.

items.q: What is an emic perspective in the study of religion?
items.correct: The insider's own understanding of a practice
items.why: It's The insider's own understanding of a practice. “The outsider's academic analysis” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which of these is a primary source for investigating a religious practice?
items.correct: An interview with a worshipper at the site
items.misconception: A textbook chapter on the religion
items.why: Correct answer: An interview with a worshipper at the site. “A textbook chapter on the religion” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is an essential ethical step when interviewing members of a religious community?
items.random_wrong: Paying them to give favourable answers
items.why: Correct answer: Obtaining their informed consent. “Recording them secretly for accuracy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A phenomenological approach to a ritual requires the researcher to do what?
items.correct: Suspend personal judgements and describe the practice as experienced
items.misconception: Prove the ritual's beliefs are true
items.random_wrong: Convert to the religion being studied
items.why: Correct answer: Suspend personal judgements and describe the practice as experienced. “Prove the ritual's beliefs are true” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
